sections.summary: Guidance for production and handling of cocoa beans for human consumption noting ochratoxin A (OTA) is a possible human carcinogen (IARC group 2B) with a JECFA PTWI of 100 ng/kg bw, produced by specific Aspergillus species under favorable water‑activity, nutrient and temperature conditions, and that improper fermentation/drying can enable OTA formation while industrial shell removal can significantly reduce OTA so industry should establish food‑safety programs.

Collection of definitions for cocoa fruit parts and quality/defect terms, including cocoa bean, pod, episperm/shell, pulp, dry cocoa, and defect classifications (mouldy, slaty, insect‑damaged, germinated, flat, smoky, broken, fragment, piece of shell), plus terms on adulteration, foreign matter and basic processing operations (harvesting/opening, fermentation, drying, sorting, roasting).

Definitions of parts of the cocoa fruit (cocoa bean, pod, episperm/shell, pulp), the commercial term "dry cocoa," numerous bean defect classifications (mouldy, slaty, insect‑damaged, germinated, flat, smoky, broken, fragment, piece of shell), and brief definitions of adulteration, foreign matter and standard processing operations (harvesting/opening, fermentation, drying, sorting, roasting).

Requirements for pod opening and initial handling: open pods manually or mechanically as soon as possible (recommended within 7 days of harvest) using clean, sharpened tools and good personal hygiene; wounded or damaged fruits should not be stored longer than one day before opening/fermenting, and defective parts and mouldy/diseased beans must be removed and disposed of while good beans are kept clean during transport to fermentation to avoid contamination.

Fermentation guidance: place pulpy beans in clean, dry suitable containers and avoid water contact; turn the mass frequently, ferment typically 4–7 days (avoid beyond 7 days to prevent fungal growth and germination), clean tools and discard unsuitable organic materials, and note fermentation generally inhibits ochratoxigenic fungi via organic acids though certain practices (e.g. fermentation on drying mats or partial depulping) may increase OTA production.

Drying requirements and limits: dry fermented beans on elevated, clean surfaces (not bare ground), by sun or artificial means until moisture is below 8% to prevent microbial growth; do not exceed a 6 cm layer (≈40 kg wet beans/m2), turn beans frequently (5–10 times/day), protect from rain/dew and animals, avoid mixing drying stages or re‑wetting (discard mouldy beans), and maintain clean drying equipment and tools.
